translations.literal: I encountered Heaven's corvee commission; greatly I loosed hardship from my person; for the sake of the infant I did not personally spare myself.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven laid this burden on me; I lifted the peril from my own shoulders for the child's sake and thought nothing of myself.

sentences.zh: 予義彼國君泉陵侯上書曰：「成王幼弱，周公踐天子位以治天下，六年，朝諸侯於明堂，制禮樂，班度量，而天下大服。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Quanling submitted a memorial, saying: "King Cheng was young and weak; the Duke of Zhou trod the Son of Heaven's position to govern the realm; in the sixth year he met the feudal lords in the Bright Hall, regulated ritual and music, promulgated measures and weights, and the realm greatly submitted."
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Quanling (Liu Yi) wrote: "When King Cheng was a child, the Duke of Zhou took the throne in all but name to rule the world; within six years he received the lords in the Bright Hall, fixed ritual and music, and standardized weights and measures—and the empire submitted."

sentences.zh: 太皇太后承順天心，成居攝之義。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager received and complied with Heaven's heart, completing the righteousness of acting as regent.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Empress Dowager followed Heaven's mind and perfected the principle of the regency.

sentences.zh: 皇太子為孝平皇帝子，年在襁褓，宜且為子，知為人子道，令皇太后得加慈母恩。

translations.literal: The imperial heir apparent is Emperor Xiaoping's son; his years are in swaddling bands; it is fitting for now to be a son, to know the way of being someone's son, and let the empress dowager be able to add a loving mother's grace.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince is Emperor Ping's infant son: let him remain simply a son, learning filial duty while the empress dowager may shower on him a mother's love.

sentences.zh: 畜養成就，加元服，然後復子明辟。」

translations.literal: Rear and nurture him to completion, add the capping, afterward restore the son to bright rule."
translations.idiomatic: Raise him to manhood, cap him when he comes of age, then return the clear Mandate to your sovereign."

sentences.zh: 熙！

translations.literal: Oh!
translations.idiomatic: Ah!

sentences.zh: 為我孺子之故，予惟趙、傅、丁、董之亂，遏絕繼嗣，變剝適、庶，危亂漢朝，以成三厄，隊極厥命。

translations.literal: For my infant's sake, I consider the disorder of Zhao, Fu, Ding, and Dong—they cut off heirs, altered and stripped heir and concubine-born, endangered and disordered the Han court, thereby completing three calamities, and ruined to the limit their mandate.
translations.idiomatic: For the child's sake I recall how the Zhao, Fu, Ding, and Dong cabals strangled the succession, muddled the lines between heir and bastard, and nearly wrecked the Han—threefold disaster that brought their houses to ruin.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 害其可不旅力同心戒之哉！

translations.literal: Harm—how can we not exert strength with one heart and guard against it!
translations.idiomatic: Can we not muster every nerve in common vigilance against such harm?

sentences.zh: 予不敢僭上帝命。

translations.literal: I dare not usurp Supreme Lord's command.
translations.idiomatic: I dare not usurp the command of High Heaven.

sentences.zh: 天休於安帝室，興我漢國，惟卜用克綏受茲命。

translations.literal: Heaven's grace rests on settling the imperial house, raises our Han state; only by using divination can we pacify and receive this mandate.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven blesses the imperial house and revives the Han; only by heeding the tortoise may we win peace and accept this charge.

sentences.zh: 今天其相民，況亦惟卜用！

translations.literal: Now Heaven assists the people—how much more must we rely on divination!
translations.idiomatic: If Heaven already favors the people, how much more must we trust the oracle!

sentences.zh: 太皇太后肇有元城沙鹿之右，陰精女主聖明之祥，配元生成，以興我天下之符，遂獲西王母之應，神靈之征，以祐我帝室，以安我大宗，以紹我後嗣，以繼我漢功。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager began with the blessing at Shalu right of Yuancheng—an omen of the yin essence female ruler's sagely clarity; she matched the primordial and bore life, thereby to raise the talisman of our realm; then she obtained the response of the Queen Mother of the West, the sign of spirits and gods, to bless our imperial house, to secure our great lineage, to continue our posterity, to carry on our Han merit.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Empress Dowager first received Heaven's favor at Shalu beside Yuancheng—a portent of the wise matriarch's yin power—joined the cosmic origin in bearing life, and so raised the charter of the empire; she then drew the response of the Queen Mother of the West, proof of divine favor, to guard the throne, steady the great clan, renew the succession, and extend Han achievement.

sentences.zh: 厥害適統不宗元緒者，辟不違親，辜不避戚。

translations.literal: As for those who harm the rightful succession and do not honor the primordial thread—punishment does not shrink from kin, guilt does not avoid in-laws.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever would wreck the true line of succession and spurn the founding thread must be punished without respect to blood or marriage.

sentences.zh: 夫豈不愛？

translations.literal: How could there be no love?
translations.idiomatic: Do you think I feel no tenderness?

sentences.zh: 亦唯帝室。

translations.literal: It is only for the imperial house.
translations.idiomatic: It is all for the sake of the imperial house.

sentences.zh: 是以廣立王侯，並建曾玄，俾屏我京師，綏撫宇內；

translations.literal: Therefore we broadly established kings and marquises, together set up great-great-grandsons, so as to screen our capital and soothe all within the seas;
translations.idiomatic: So she enfeoffed kings and marquises far and wide, ennobled descendants to the fifth generation, to shield the capital and bring peace within the four seas;

sentences.zh: 博征儒生，講道於廷，論序乖繆，制禮作樂，同律度量，混一風俗；

translations.literal: widely summoned scholars, expounded the Way in court, discussed and ordered what was wrong, made ritual and composed music, unified pitch-pipes and measures, merged customs into one;
translations.idiomatic: summoned Confucians from every quarter, debated the classics in the hall, corrected error, codified ritual and music, aligned pitch and weights, and knit the customs of the realm into one;

sentences.zh: 正天地之位，昭郊宗之禮，定五畤廟祧，咸秩亡文；

translations.literal: rectified the positions of Heaven and Earth, clarified suburban and ancestral rites, fixed the five shrines and temple remote altars, all ranked what had lacked texts;
translations.idiomatic: set right cosmic order, made clear suburban and temple worship, regulated the five altars and distant shrines, and gave written rank to every neglected rite;

sentences.zh: 建靈台，立明堂，設辟雍，張太學，尊中宗、高宗之號。

translations.literal: built the Spirit Terrace, erected the Bright Hall, set up the Ring Hall, spread the Imperial Academy, honored the titles of Middle and High Ancestors.
translations.idiomatic: raised the Spirit Terrace and Bright Hall, founded the Ring Hall and Imperial Academy, and restored the canonical titles of Zhongzong and Gaozong.

sentences.zh: 昔我高宗崇德建武，克綏西域，以受白虎威勝之瑞，天地判合，乾、坤序德。

translations.literal: Anciently our High Ancestor exalted virtue and established martial might, was able to pacify the Western Regions, and received the auspice of the white tiger of awesome victory; Heaven and Earth divided and joined, Qian and Kun ordered their powers.
translations.idiomatic: Our High Ancestor exalted civil virtue and forged military power until the Western Regions submitted and the white tiger omen of conquest appeared; Heaven and earth aligned and the cosmic forces fell into moral order.

sentences.zh: 太皇太后臨政，有龜、龍、麟、鳳之應，五德嘉符，相因而備。

translations.literal: When the Grand Empress Dowager faced the government, there were responses of tortoise, dragon, unicorn, and phoenix; the five virtues' fine talismans followed one another and were complete.
translations.idiomatic: Under her rule came omens of tortoise, dragon, unicorn, and phoenix, and the five phases' blessed tokens arrived in succession until none was missing.

sentences.zh: 河圖、洛書遠自崑崙，出於重野。

translations.literal: The River Chart and Luo Writing came from far Kunlun, issued from layered wilds.
translations.idiomatic: The River Chart and Luo Writing appeared, said to come from Kunlun and the deep hinterland.

sentences.zh: 古讖著言，肆今享實。

translations.literal: Ancient prophecies set forth words; now we enjoy their truth.
translations.idiomatic: Old prognostic texts foretold these things; today we see them fulfilled in fact.

sentences.zh: 此乃皇天上帝所以安我帝室，俾我成就洪烈也。

translations.literal: This is how August Heaven and Supreme Lord settle our imperial house and let me complete the great enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: Thus High Heaven steadies the throne and lets me finish the great work entrusted to the Han.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 天明威輔漢始而大大矣。

translations.literal: Heaven's bright awe aids the Han from the beginning—how greatly!
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's majestic favor has upheld the Han from the first—how vast it has grown!

sentences.zh: 爾有惟舊人泉陵侯之言，爾不克遠省，爾豈知太皇太后若此勤哉！

translations.literal: You have the words of the old man Marquis of Quanling—if you cannot reflect from afar, how could you know the Grand Empress Dowager labors thus!
translations.idiomatic: You heard the Marquis of Quanling's memorial—if you will not think back, can you grasp how tirelessly the Grand Empress Dowager has toiled?

sentences.zh: 天毖勞我成功所，予不敢不極卒安皇帝之所圖事。

translations.literal: Heaven labors me at the place of success's completion; I dare not fail to exhaust to the utmost settling what the August Emperor planned.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven drives me to finish the work; I dare not fail to carry through what the late emperor intended for the peace of the realm.

sentences.zh: 肆予告我諸侯王公、列侯、卿、大夫、元士御事：天輔誠辭，天其累我以民，予害敢不於祖宗安人圖功所終？

translations.literal: Therefore I declare to my feudal kings and dukes, full marquises, ministers, great officers, and grandees charged with affairs: Heaven aids sincere words; Heaven may burden me with the people—how dare I not, for the ancestors, settle the people and plan the end of merit?
translations.idiomatic: Hear me, kings, marquises, ministers, and all who govern: Heaven favors honest counsel and may load me with the people's weal—how could I refuse, for the ancestors' sake, to secure the folk and finish the task?

sentences.zh: 天亦惟勞我民，若有疾，予害敢不於祖宗所受休輔？

translations.literal: Heaven also labors my people as if with sickness—how dare I not use the restful aid received from the ancestors?
translations.idiomatic: Heaven troubles the people like a lingering illness—how dare I set aside the blessing the ancestors won for us?

sentences.zh: 予聞孝子善繼人之意，忠臣善成人之事。

translations.literal: I have heard that filial sons well continue others' intents, and loyal ministers well complete others' affairs.
translations.idiomatic: They say a good son carries on a father's purpose, and a loyal minister completes his lord's enterprise.

sentences.zh: 予思若考作室，厥子堂而構之；

translations.literal: I think: if the father builds a house, the son raises the hall and frames it;
translations.idiomatic: When the father lays a foundation, the son raises the beams and roof;

sentences.zh: 厥父菑，厥子播而獲之。

translations.literal: if the father clears new fields, the son sows and reaps.
translations.idiomatic: when the father clears the ground, the son sows and gathers the harvest.

sentences.zh: 予害敢不於身撫祖宗之所受大命？

translations.literal: How dare I not with my person soothe the great mandate received from the ancestors?
translations.idiomatic: How dare I not embrace with my own body the great charge the ancestors received?

sentences.zh: 若祖宗乃有效湯、武伐厥子，民長其勸弗救。

translations.literal: If the ancestors then had Tang and Wu punish their sons, the people would long encourage and not save.
translations.idiomatic: Had the ancestors called down a Tang- or Wu-style punishment on rebellious sons, the people would have cheered and offered no rescue.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼肆哉！

translations.literal: Alas, how stern!
translations.idiomatic: So be it—Heaven's severity is plain!

sentences.zh: 諸侯王公、列侯、卿、大夫、元士御事，其勉助國道明！

translations.literal: Feudal kings and dukes, full marquises, ministers, great officers, and grandees charged with affairs—strive to aid the state and clarify the Way!
translations.idiomatic: All you kings, marquises, ministers, and officials—exert yourselves to support the state and make the Way clear!

sentences.zh: 亦惟宗室之俊，民之表儀，迪知上帝命。

translations.literal: Also you who are the fine of the imperial clan, the people's model, who enlighten and know Supreme Lord's command.
translations.idiomatic: You who are the flower of the Liu house and the people's example—open your hearts to Heaven's command.

sentences.zh: 粵天輔誠，爾不得易定！

translations.literal: Thus Heaven aids sincerity—you may not lightly change what is fixed!
translations.idiomatic: Heaven rewards good faith—you must not lightly overturn what is settled!

sentences.zh: 況今天降定於漢國，惟大艱人翟義、劉信大逆，欲相伐於厥室，豈亦知命之不易乎？

translations.literal: How much more now that Heaven has sent down settlement on the Han state—only the greatly wicked men Zhai Yi and Liu Xin, in great rebellion, wish to strike one another in their chamber—do they also know the mandate is not easy?
translations.idiomatic: Now that Heaven has fixed the Han's fate, Zhai Yi and Liu Xin—villains of the worst sort—would tear the house apart from within. Do they imagine the Mandate comes cheaply?

sentences.zh: 予永念曰天惟喪翟義、劉信，若嗇夫，予害敢不終予畝？

translations.literal: I ever ponder: Heaven will destroy Zhai Yi and Liu Xin; like a miser—how dare I not finish my furrows?
translations.idiomatic: I reflect that Heaven means to destroy Yi and Xin; like a farmer at harvest, how dare I leave a single row untilled?

sentences.zh: 天亦惟休於祖宗，予害其極卜，害敢不於從？

translations.literal: Heaven also rests grace on the ancestors—how could I exhaust divination? How dare I not follow?
translations.idiomatic: Heaven has smiled on the ancestors too—why would I doubt the oracle further? How could I refuse to obey?

sentences.zh: 率寧人有旨疆土，況今卜並吉！

translations.literal: Lead the settled people to possess fine territory—how much more now that divination is all auspicious!
translations.idiomatic: Lead the people to secure the land—especially when every cast of the shells has come back favorable!

sentences.zh: 故予大以爾東征，命不僭差，卜陳惟若此。

translations.literal: Therefore I greatly use you to march east; the command is not usurped or awry—the tortoise's display is only thus.
translations.idiomatic: Therefore I send you on the eastern campaign; the order matches the oracle and brooks no tampering—the shells have spoken plainly.

sentences.zh: 乃遣大夫桓譚等班行諭告當反立孺子之意。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent great officers such as Huan Tan to circulate and proclaim the intent that the infant heir should be restored.
translations.idiomatic: He then dispatched Huan Tan and other senior officials on circuit to explain that power would return to the boy emperor.

sentences.zh: 還，封譚為明告裡附城。

translations.literal: When they returned, Tan was enfeoffed as attached-to-walled-city at Bright-Proclaim Hamlet.
translations.idiomatic: On their return Huan Tan was ennobled as a ranked noble of the Bright-Proclaim precinct.

sentences.zh: 諸將東至陳留菑，與義會戰，破之，斬劉璜首。

translations.literal: The generals went east to Zhi in Chenliu, joined battle with Yi, defeated him, and beheaded Liu Huang.
translations.idiomatic: The imperial host reached Zhi in Chenliu, smashed Yi's army, and sent Liu Huang's head to the camp.

sentences.zh: 莽大喜，復下詔曰：

translations.literal: Mang was greatly pleased and again issued an edict, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang rejoiced and promulgated another edict:

sentences.zh: 太皇太后遭家不造，國統三絕，絕輒復續，恩莫厚焉，信莫立焉。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager met a house not made; the dynastic thread thrice broke, and each break was renewed—grace could not be thicker, trust could not be more established.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Empress Dowager endured a house struck by repeated tragedy: the succession failed three times, yet each time was mended—no kindness could run deeper, no bond of trust stand firmer.

sentences.zh: 孝平皇帝短命蚤崩，幼嗣孺沖，詔予居攝。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoping had a short life and died early; the young heir was an infant; she edicted me to act as regent.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ping died young, leaving only an infant heir; she ordered me to take the regency.

sentences.zh: 予承明詔，奉社稷之任，持大宗之重，養六尺之托，受天下之寄，戰戰兢兢，不敢安息。

translations.literal: I received the clear edict, bore the altars' charge, held the great lineage's weight, nurtured the six-foot charge, received the realm's trust—trembling, I did not dare rest.
translations.idiomatic: I accepted the patent, shouldered the altars, bore the weight of the imperial line, guarded the child entrusted to me, and took the world's burden—always in fear, never at ease.

sentences.zh: 伏念太皇太后惟經藝分析，王道離散，漢家制作之業獨未成就，故博征儒士，大興典制，備物致用，立功成器，以為天下利。

translations.literal: Bowing, I thought: the Grand Empress Dowager because the classics were split apart and the kingly way scattered, and the Han house's work of establishment alone was not yet achieved, therefore broadly summoned Confucians, greatly raised statutes and norms, prepared things and extended use, set merit and completed tools, to profit the realm.
translations.idiomatic: I reflected that she saw the classics in fragments and royal governance adrift, while Han institutions remained unfinished; so she summoned scholars everywhere, rebuilt statutes, furnished implements, founded lasting works, and aimed at good for all under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 王道粲然，基業既著，千載之廢，百世之遺，於今乃成，道德庶幾於唐、虞，功烈比齊於殷、周。

translations.literal: The kingly way shines bright; the foundation is already manifest; what a thousand years abandoned and a hundred generations lost is now accomplished—virtue nearly matches Tang and Yu; merit ranks with Yin and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The royal Way now gleams; the foundations stand fast; what lay in ruin for ages is restored—our virtue approaches Yao and Shun, our deeds rival Yin and Zhou.

sentences.zh: 今翟義、劉信等謀反大逆，流言惑眾，欲以篡位，賊害我孺子，罪深於管、蔡，惡甚於禽獸。

translations.literal: Now Zhai Yi, Liu Xin, and others plotted rebellion in great treason, spread rumors to delude the masses, wished thereby to usurp the throne, and murdered my infant—their guilt is deeper than Guan and Cai, their wickedness worse than birds and beasts.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Yi and Liu Xin conspired in the foulest treason, whispered lies to the crowd, sought the throne, and would have killed the boy heir—their crime outdoes Guan and Cai, their nature is viler than beasts.

sentences.zh: 信父故東平王雲，不孝不謹，親毒殺其父思王，名曰巨鼠，後雲竟坐大逆誅死。

translations.literal: Xin's father, the former king of Dongping Liu Yun, was unfilial and uncareful, personally poison-killed his father King Si—he was called 'great rat'; afterward Yun finally sat for great treason and was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin's father, the late King Yun of Dongping, lacked filial piety: he poisoned his own father King Si and was nicknamed the giant rat—later he was executed for high treason.

sentences.zh: 義父故丞相方進，險波陰賊，兄宣靜言令色，外巧內嫉，所殺鄉邑汝南者數十人。

translations.literal: Yi's father, the former chancellor Fangjin, was treacherous and crooked; his elder brother Xuan had quiet words and pleasing looks, outwardly clever but inwardly jealous—those killed in villages of Runan numbered several tens.
translations.idiomatic: Yi's father, the late Chancellor Zhai Fangjin, was devious and cruel; his brother Xuan wore a smooth face while nursing envy, and dozens died by their hands in Runan townships.

sentences.zh: 今積惡二家，迷惑相得，此時命當殄。

translations.literal: Now the two houses accumulated wickedness, deluded and matched each other—at this time fate ought to be extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Two families steeped in evil have joined in folly—their hour of doom has come.

sentences.zh: 天所滅也。

translations.literal: It is what Heaven destroys.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven itself has marked them for destruction.

sentences.zh: 義始發兵，上書言宇、信等與東平相輔謀反，執捕械系，欲以威民，先自相被以反逆大惡，轉相捕械，此其破殄之明證也。

translations.literal: When Yi first raised troops, he memorialized saying Yu, Xin, and others plotted rebellion with the Dongping chancellor; he seized and shackled them, wishing thereby to awe the people—first he himself draped them in the great wickedness of rebellion, then mutually seized and shackled—this is clear proof of their defeat and extinction.
translations.idiomatic: When Yi first rose he denounced Liu Yu and Liu Xin as fellow plotters with the Dongping chancellor, clapped them in irons to cow the people—then turned the same charge on one another in a chain of arrests: plain proof their league was already breaking apart.

sentences.zh: 已捕斬斷信二子穀鄉侯章、德廣侯鮪，義母練、兄宣、親屬二十四人皆磔暴於長安都市四通之衢。

translations.literal: Already captured and beheaded were Xin's two sons—Marquis of Guxiang Zhang and Marquis of Deguang Wei; Yi's mother Lian, elder brother Xuan, and twenty-four kin were all torn apart and exposed at the four-way crossroads of Chang'an's market wards.
translations.idiomatic: Xin's sons, Marquis Zhang of Guxiang and Marquis Wei of Deguang, were caught and executed; Yi's mother Lian, brother Xuan, and twenty-four relatives were publicly dismembered at the great crossroads of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 當其斬時，觀者重疊，天氣和清，可謂當矣。

translations.literal: At the time of their beheading, watchers piled layer on layer; the weather was mild and clear—it may be called fitting.
translations.idiomatic: When the blades fell the crowds were stacked deep, yet the sky stayed clear and mild—as if Heaven approved the season.

sentences.zh: 命遣大將軍共行皇天之罰，討誨內之仇，功效著焉，予甚嘉之。

translations.literal: I ordered the grand general jointly to carry out August Heaven's punishment, to punish the hatred within the realm—merit and effect were manifest; I greatly praise it.
translations.idiomatic: I sent the grand general to execute Heaven's sentence on rebels within the borders—the victory was plain, and I applaud it.

sentences.zh: 《司馬法》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does the "Marshal's Methods" not say?
translations.idiomatic: Has not the "Marshal's Methods" said?

sentences.zh: 「賞不逾時」。

translations.literal: "Rewards do not pass the proper season."
translations.idiomatic: "Bestow rewards before the season turns."

sentences.zh: 欲民速睹為善之利也。

translations.literal: It means wishing the people quickly to see the benefit of doing good.
translations.idiomatic: So the people may swiftly see the profit in virtue.

sentences.zh: 今先封車騎都尉孫賢等五十五人皆為列侯，戶邑之數別下。

translations.literal: Now first enfeoff chariot-and-cavalry commandant Sun Xian and fifty-five others all as full marquises; the numbers of households and fiefs will be issued separately.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Xian, chariot-and-cavalry commandant, and fifty-five others are now raised to full marquisates; patent rolls with fief sizes will follow.

sentences.zh: 遣使者持黃金印、赤紱縌、硃輪車，即軍中拜授。

translations.literal: Dispatch envoys bearing gold seals, crimson ribbons, and vermillion-wheeled chariots to invest them in camp.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys will ride out with gold seals, crimson cords, and cinnabar-wheeled carriages to confer the titles in the field.

sentences.zh: 因大赦天下。

translations.literal: Thereupon he greatly pardoned the realm.
translations.idiomatic: He then proclaimed a general amnesty.

sentences.zh: 於是吏士精銳遂功圍義於圉城，破之，義與劉信棄軍庸亡。

translations.literal: Thereupon crack officers and troops pressed the siege on Yi at Yucheng, broke him, and Yi with Liu Xin abandoned the army and fled in panic.
translations.idiomatic: Elite troops then besieged Yi at Yucheng, broke his lines, and Yi fled with Liu Xin, throwing away their host.

sentences.zh: 至固始界中捕得義，屍磔陳都市。

translations.literal: Reaching the border of Gushi they captured Yi; his corpse was torn apart and displayed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: They ran him down on the border of Gushi; his body was quartered and hung in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 卒不得信。

translations.literal: In the end they did not obtain Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin was never taken.

sentences.zh: 初，三輔聞翟義起，自茂陵以西至□二十三縣盜賊並發，趙明、霍鴻等自稱將軍，攻燒官寺，殺右輔都尉及□令，劫略吏民，眾十餘萬，火見未央宮前殿。

translations.literal: At first, when the Three Adjuncts heard Zhai Yi had risen, from Maoling west to □ twenty-three counties bandits rose together; Zhao Ming, Huo Hong, and others styled themselves generals, attacked and burned government offices, killed the capital-region deputy and □ magistrate, plundered officials and commoners—the host was more than a hundred thousand; fire was seen before Weiyang Palace's front hall.
translations.idiomatic: When Yi rebelled, from Maoling west through twenty-three counties the capital region erupted: Zhao Ming and Huo Hong declared themselves generals, torched yamen, slew metropolitan deputies and magistrates, looted towns, and gathered more than a hundred thousand men until flames lit the sky before Weiyang Palace.

sentences.zh: 莽晝夜抱孺子禱宗廟。

translations.literal: Mang day and night embraced the infant and prayed in the ancestral shrines.
translations.idiomatic: Mang day and night clutched the boy emperor and prayed in the shrines of his ancestors.

sentences.zh: 復拜衛尉王級為虎賁將軍，大鴻臚望鄉侯閻遷為折衝將軍，與甄邯、王晏西擊趙明等。

translations.literal: Again he appointed Commandant of the Guards Wang Ji as Tiger Fury general, Grand Herald Marquis of Wangxiang Yan Qian as Assault general, together with Zhen Han and Wang Yan to strike Zhao Ming and others from the west.
translations.idiomatic: He named Wang Ji, commandant of the guards, Tiger Fury general, and Yan Qian, Marquis of Wangxiang, as Assault general, and sent them with Zhen Han and Wang Yan west against Zhao Ming.

sentences.zh: 正月，虎牙將軍王邑等自關東還，便引兵西。

translations.literal: In the first month, Tiger Fang general Wang Yi and others returned from east of the passes and thereupon led troops west.
translations.idiomatic: In the first month Wang Yi, Tiger Fang general, marched back from the east and at once turned his column west.

sentences.zh: 強弩將軍王駿以無功免，揚武將軍劉歆歸故官。

translations.literal: Strong Crossbow general Wang Jun was dismissed for lacking merit; General Who Displays Martial Prowess Liu Xin returned to his former office.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jun lost his post as Strong Crossbow general for failure; Liu Xin went back to his old portfolio as general displaying martial prowess.

sentences.zh: 復以邑弟侍中王奇為揚武將軍，城門將軍趙恢為強弩將軍，中郎將李□為厭難將軍，復將兵西。

translations.literal: Again he made Yi's younger brother, attendant Wang Qi, General Who Displays Martial Prowess, Gate general Zhao Hui Strong Crossbow general, Gentleman of the Palace Li □ Hardship-quelling general, and again led troops west.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yi's brother Wang Qi, a palace attendant, was named general displaying martial prowess; Zhao Hui became Strong Crossbow general; a Gentleman Li □ took the Hardship-quelling title, and the western column marched again.

sentences.zh: 二月，明等殄滅，諸縣悉平，還師振旅。

translations.literal: In the second month Ming and the others were wiped out; all counties were pacified; the army returned and shook the ranks.
translations.idiomatic: By the second month Zhao Ming's band was destroyed, every county was quiet, and the host marched home in good order.

sentences.zh: 莽乃置酒白虎殿，勞饗將帥，大封拜。

translations.literal: Mang thereupon set wine in the White Tiger Hall, feasted and rewarded generals and commanders, and greatly invested and appointed.
translations.idiomatic: Mang then held a banquet in the White Tiger Hall for his generals, heaped rewards on them, and handed out a fresh round of titles.

sentences.zh: 先是，益州蠻夷及金城塞外羌反畔，時州郡擊破之。

translations.literal: Earlier, the barbarians of Yi commandery and the Qiang beyond the barrier of Jincheng had rebelled; at the time commanderies and states had crushed them.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier the Yi tribesmen and the Jincheng Qiang beyond the wall had risen; provincial troops had already beaten them down.

sentences.zh: 莽乃並隸，以小大為差，封侯、伯、子、男凡三百九十五人，曰「皆以奮怒，東指西擊，羌寇蠻盜，反虜逆賊，不得旋踵，應時殄滅，天下咸服」之功封雲。

translations.literal: Mang thereupon combined and subsumed them, by great and small as gradation, enfeoffing marquises, earls, viscounts, and barons—three hundred ninety-five persons in all—saying "all by raging anger, pointing east and striking west, Qiang raiders and Man bandits, rebel captives and traitorous thieves, could not turn heel; they were wiped out in season, and the realm all submitted"—merit for enfeoffment was thus declared.
translations.idiomatic: Mang rolled every exploit into one list and ennobled 395 men as marquises, earls, viscounts, or barons, citing their fury in battle—striking east and west, crushing Qiang and Man, rebels who could not escape—and claiming universal submission as their warrant.

sentences.zh: 莽於是自謂大得天人之助，至其年十二月，遂即真矣。

translations.literal: Mang thereupon deemed himself greatly to have obtained Heaven and man's aid; reaching that year's twelfth month, he thereupon assumed the true position.
translations.idiomatic: Convinced that Heaven and humanity favored him, in the twelfth month of that year he took the throne in his own name.

sentences.zh: 初，義所收宛令劉立聞義舉兵，上書願備軍吏為國討賊，內報私怨。

translations.literal: At first, Wan magistrate Liu Li whom Yi had arrested, hearing Yi had raised troops, submitted a memorial wishing to serve as army clerk to punish rebels for the state—inwardly to repay private resentment.
translations.idiomatic: The Wan magistrate Liu Li, whom Yi had once jailed, offered to enlist against the rebels—chiefly to settle a private score.

sentences.zh: 莽擢立為陳留太守，封明德侯。

translations.literal: Mang promoted Li to grand warden of Chenliu and enfeoffed him as Marquis of Bright Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Mang raised him to grand warden of Chenliu and made him Marquis of Bright Virtue.

sentences.zh: 始，義兄宣居長安，先義未發，家數有怪，夜聞哭聲，聽之不知所在。

translations.literal: At the beginning, Yi's elder brother Xuan dwelt in Chang'an; before Yi had yet launched, the house several times had strange things—at night they heard weeping sounds, listening without knowing where they were.
translations.idiomatic: Before the rising, Yi's brother Xuan lived in Chang'an; the house was plagued by omens and night weeping with no visible source.

sentences.zh: 宣教授諸生滿堂，有狗從外入，嚙其中庭群雁數十，比驚救之，已皆斷頭。

translations.literal: Xuan was lecturing students filling the hall when a dog entered from outside and bit several tens of geese in the middle courtyard; by the time they were startled and rescued them, all had severed heads.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan was lecturing to a full hall when a dog burst in and tore the heads from dozens of geese in the courtyard before anyone could stop it.

sentences.zh: 狗走出門，求不知處。

translations.literal: The dog went out the gate; they sought it without finding where it was.
translations.idiomatic: The dog vanished through the gate and could not be found.

sentences.zh: 宣大惡之，謂後母曰：「東郡太守文仲素□儻，今數有惡怪，恐有妄為而大禍至也。

translations.literal: Xuan greatly hated it and said to his stepmother: "The grand warden of Dong commandery, Wenzhong, is by nature □ daring; now several times there have been evil prodigies—I fear there will be rash acts and great calamity will arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan was deeply troubled and told his stepmother: "My brother Wenzhong, grand warden of Dong, is reckless by nature; these omens foretell rash action and great disaster."

sentences.zh: 大夫人可歸，為棄去宣家者以避害。」

translations.literal: Great lady, you may return home, acting as one who abandons and leaves the Xuan household so as to avoid harm."
translations.idiomatic: Mother, you should go home and sever ties with our house to escape what is coming."

sentences.zh: 母不肯去，後數月敗。

translations.literal: The mother was unwilling to go; after several months they were defeated.
translations.idiomatic: She refused to leave; within months the clan was ruined.

sentences.zh: 莽盡壞義第宅，污池之。

translations.literal: Mang utterly destroyed Yi's residences and turned them into foul ponds.
translations.idiomatic: Mang razed Yi's mansions and drowned the foundations in cesspools.

sentences.zh: 發父方進及先祖塚在汝南者，燒其棺柩，夷滅三族，誅及種嗣，至皆同坑，以棘五毒並葬之。

translations.literal: He dug up Yi's father Fangjin and ancestors' tombs in Runan, burned their coffins, exterminated three clans, executed down to seed and heirs—all together in one pit, burying them with thorn and the five poisons together.
translations.idiomatic: He opened the graves of Fangjin and the Zhai line in Runan, burned the coffins, extirpated three degrees of kin to the last child, and flung them into a common pit strewn with thorns and mingled with the five poisons.

sentences.zh: 而下詔曰：「蓋聞古者伐不敬，取其鯨鯢築武軍，封以為大戮，於是乎有京觀以懲淫□。

translations.literal: And he issued an edict saying: "I have heard that in antiquity when punishing disrespect they took the whale and ni, built martial mounds, sealed them as great executions—thereby they had capital mounds to punish wanton □.
translations.idiomatic: He proclaimed: "The ancients executed the worst criminals, heaped their bones into warning mounds, and sealed them as monuments against wanton rebellion.

sentences.zh: 乃者反虜劉信、翟義悖逆作亂於東，而芒竹群盜趙明、霍鴻造逆西土，遣武將征討，咸伏其辜。

translations.literal: Lately the rebel captives Liu Xin and Zhai Yi acted contrary and raised chaos in the east, while Mangzhu bandit hosts Zhao Ming and Huo Hong made rebellion in the western lands; I dispatched martial generals to punish and attack, and all submitted to their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Lately Liu Xin and Zhai Yi rebelled in the east while Zhao Ming and Huo Hong raided the capital region; I sent generals who brought every one of them to book."

sentences.zh: 惟信、義等始發自濮陽，結奸無鹽，殄滅於圉。

translations.literal: As for Xin, Yi, and others, they began from Puyang, knotted wickedness at Wuyan, and were extinguished at Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Yi and his allies began at Puyang, joined forces at Wuyan, and were destroyed at Yu.

sentences.zh: 趙明依阻槐裡環堤，霍鴻負倚盩厔芒竹，咸用破碎，亡有餘類。

translations.literal: Zhao Ming relied on the barrier of Huaili's ring dike; Huo Hong leaned on Zhizhou's Mangzhu—all were smashed to pieces, with no remnant kind left.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ming held the ring dike at Huaili; Huo Hong used the bamboo thickets at Zhouzhi—both were shattered without survivors.

sentences.zh: 其取反虜逆賊之鯨鯢，聚之通路之旁，濮陽、無鹽、圉、槐裡、□凡五所，各方六丈，高六尺，築為武軍，封以為大戮，薦樹之棘。

translations.literal: Take the whale-ni of rebel captives and traitorous thieves, gather them beside the through-roads—Puyang, Wuyan, Yu, Huaili, □ in all five places, each place six fathoms square, six feet high, built as martial mounds, sealed as great executions, with thorn planted on top.
translations.idiomatic: Heap the corpses of the chief rebels at five crossroads—Puyang, Wuyan, Yu, Huaili, and one other—each mound six paces square and six feet high, crowned with thorns as a warning to posterity.

sentences.zh: 建表木，高丈六尺。

translations.literal: Erect marker posts, height one rod six feet.
translations.idiomatic: Raise marker posts sixteen feet high.

sentences.zh: 書曰『反虜逆賊鯨鯢』，在所長吏常以秋循行，勿令壞敗，以懲淫□焉。」

translations.literal: The inscription reads "Rebel captives and traitorous thieves, whale-ni"; the local chief officers in autumn shall patrol and not allow ruin, to punish wanton □."
translations.idiomatic: Inscribe them "Whale-ni of treason"; each autumn the magistrate shall inspect the mounds and keep them intact, a standing lesson against rebellion."

sentences.zh: 初，汝南舊有鴻隙大陂，郡以為饒，成帝時，關東數水，陂溢為害。

translations.literal: At first Runan formerly had the great marsh of Hongxi; the commandery deemed it rich; in Emperor Cheng's time east of the passes repeatedly had floods, and the marsh overflowed and became harm.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago Runan held the great Hongxi marsh, which the commandery counted a treasure; under Emperor Cheng repeated floods in the east made it spill and scour the land.

sentences.zh: 方進為相，與御史大夫孔光共遣掾行視，以為決去陂水，其地肥美，省堤防費而無水憂，遂奏罷之。

translations.literal: When Fangjin was chancellor, together with Imperial Counselor Kong Guang he sent clerks to inspect on circuit; they thought to drain off the marsh water—the land was fertile and fine, saving dike costs and removing water worry—thereupon they memorialized to abolish it.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor, Zhai Fangjin and Imperial Counselor Kong Guang sent surveyors who argued that draining Hongxi would free rich farmland, cut dike expense, and end flooding—so they memorialized to drain it.

sentences.zh: 及翟氏滅，鄉里歸惡，言方進請陂下良田不得而奏罷陂雲。

translations.literal: When the Zhai house was destroyed, the villages returned hatred, saying Fangjin memorialized to abolish the marsh because he could not obtain fine fields below the marsh.
translations.idiomatic: After the Zhai clan fell, locals spitefully claimed Fangjin had ordered the marsh drained because landowners below it had refused him their fields.

sentences.zh: 王莽時常枯旱，郡中追怨方進，童謠曰：「壞陂誰？

translations.literal: In Wang Mang's time it was often parched drought; the commandery afterward resented Fangjin; a children's rhyme said: "Who broke the marsh?
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang droughts came year after year, and Runan blamed Fangjin in a rhyme: "Who broke the dyke?

sentences.zh: 翟子威。

translations.literal: Master Ziwei.
translations.idiomatic: Ziwei of the Zhai.

sentences.zh: 飯我豆食羹芋魁。

translations.literal: He fed us bean food and taro-root gruel.
translations.idiomatic: He fed us beans and taro gruel.

sentences.zh: 反乎覆，陂當復。

translations.literal: Turned over and turned back—the marsh ought to be restored.
translations.idiomatic: When the wheel turns, the marsh will return.

sentences.zh: 誰云者？

translations.literal: Who says it?
translations.idiomatic: Who says so?

sentences.zh: 兩黃鵠。」

translations.literal: Two yellow swans."
translations.idiomatic: Two yellow swans."

sentences.zh: 司徒掾班彪曰：「丞相方進以孤童攜老母，羈旅入京師，身為儒宗，致位宰相，盛矣。

translations.literal: Supervisor of the Household clerk Ban Biao said: "Chancellor Fangjin, a lone lad carrying his old mother, wandered as a traveler into the capital; his person became the Confucians' patriarch and reached the chancellorship—how grand.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao of the Supervisor of the Household wrote: "Zhai Fangjin came to the capital a poor orphan leading his mother, rose to head the scholars, and reached the chancellorship—splendid heights.

sentences.zh: 當莽之起，蓋乘天威，雖有賁、育，奚益於敵？

translations.literal: When Mang rose, it was by riding Heaven's awesomeness; though there were Ben and Yu, what benefit against the enemy?
translations.idiomatic: Once Wang Mang moved, he rode a wave of seeming celestial mandate; even bravos the match of Meng Ben or Xia Yu could have done little against such force?

sentences.zh: 義不量力，懷忠憤發，以隕其宗，悲夫！」

translations.literal: Yi did not measure strength, harbored loyalty and angrily burst forth, thereby causing his clan to fall—how pitiful!"
translations.idiomatic: Yi misjudged his own weight, let loyal rage carry him, and brought his whole lineage to ruin—how bitter an end!"
